Applicants must submit a cover letter, curriculum vitae, and previous teaching evaluations, if 
applicable. Applications should be addressed to the Assistant Dean, Graduate Programs and

duties:
This posting is for a course instructor for Applied Legal Research and Writing; a mid-size course
offered in the Canadian Law in a Global Context concentration of the Faculty’s Global 
Professional Master of Laws program. The instructor will teach one section of this course, and 
will be responsible for performing many of the duties associated with teaching a graduate 
course, including developing and delivering course materials, lecturing, conducting office 
hours, designing and marking assignments, assessing student work and participation, 
providing student feedback, submitting grades to the Assistant Dean, Graduate Program, and 
more. The instructor is also required to attend any instructor orientation meetings. Additional 
duties may be required as directed by the Assistant or Associate Dean of the Graduate 
Programs. Please note that this course will be delivered in-person. As such, the instructor will 
be required to regularly attend the St. George campus to teach the course and meet with 
students.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than past teaching experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
This position is open to all students in the upper years of the Faculty of Law’s SJD program (e.g. 
year two and beyond). Prior teaching experience is strongly preferred. Familiarity with course 
content and knowledge of the Canadian legal system is necessary. The ability to manage 
deadlines and proven excellent communication and collaboration skills are essential. 
Experience as a practicing lawyer is preferred.
qualifications_preferred: Experience as a practicing lawyer is preferred
salery:
$9,442.98 for returning course instructors or $10,553.15 for first time course 
instructors (if there is a discrepancy between the posted salary and that required by the 
Collective Agreement, the rate provided in the Collective Agreement shall prevail).

qualifications_minimum:
An academic background in Aerospace Engineering, or Engineering Science, with a focus on spacecraft engineering, astronautics, or space systems. Prior teaching assistantship experience in aerospace systems, orbital mechanics, spacecraft design, control systems, or related courses is an asset. Graduate-level study and research experience in spacecraft engineering, space mission design, or satellite systems are preferred. 

Knowledge in Orbital Mechanics & Mission Analysis (Keplerian orbits, orbital transfers, mission planning, and the space environment), Spacecraft Systems Engineering (system architecture, subsystem integration, mass and power budgeting), Attitude Determination & Control (sensors, actuators, stabilization, and pointing control), Spacecraft Subsystems (electrical power systems, thermal control, propulsion, command and data handling), Space Communications (antennas, telemetry, link budgets, and communication architectures), and Space Structures & Mechanisms (structural design considerations, deployment mechanisms, and spacecraft mass properties). Familiarity with spacecraft modeling, simulation, and mission analysis software is considered an asset.

Knowledge in Robot Kinematics (forward, inverse, and differential kinematics, coordinate transformations, and screw theory), Robot Dynamics & Statics (force and torque analysis, inverse and forward dynamics, manipulator modeling, and actuator dynamics), Control Systems for Robotics (motion control, force control, trajectory planning, feedback control, and stability analysis), Robot Design & Manipulation (industrial manipulators, end-effectors, workspace analysis, task-based design, and actuation systems), Mobile Robotics (mobile manipulation, navigation fundamentals, and autonomous robotic systems), and Sensors & Instrumentation (encoders, IMUs, force/torque sensors, machine vision, data acquisition, and robotic system integration). application_procedure: How to Apply:                    "Please click on the "How to Apply" tab on our website: https://bloomberg.nursing.utoronto.ca/careers/
duties:
The Lawrence Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing Program provides a full-time, two-year, second entry baccalaureate nursing education. Given that nursing is a practice-based profession, we believe that clinical experience is a vital and integral component of the curriculum, providing students with hands-on opportunities to actively apply classroom learning to ‘real life’ nursing practice under the supervision and support of a clinical teacher. Clinical Instructors (CIs) play an important role in facilitating student learning to further develop students’ knowledge, skill, and judgement. CIs are expected to support both students and preceptors in placements. CIs should be flexible and prepared to utilize additional learning modalities such as simulation and virtual clinical learning in the event a clinical day is cancelled by the organization. CIs will work closely with the Clinical Education Office and Course Faculty to develop and share resources to support the continuity of clinical teaching and student learning. CIs are required to complete all on-boarding and orientation activities required by the faculty as well as the clinical placement site prior to the start of the placement in preparation for their clinical teaching role. Ongoing support will be provided throughout the term by Course Faculty, the Undergraduate Clinical Resource Faculty, and the Clinical Education Office.  Applicants should note that commitment to the role for the entire term is needed for consistency in the development, assessment, and evaluation of nursing students. Duties and Responsibilities:
1. Complete the asynchronous CI orientation learning modules as required and attend the in-person Faculty-led CI orientation for NUR461. 
2. Complete all on-boarding and orientation required by the University as well as the clinical placement sites. 
3. Familiarize themselves with and uphold all faculty and university policies related to clinical placements (e.g., CI and Student Absences, Professional Behavior). 
4. Assist students in creating clinical learning objectives for their respective community placements that are reflective of course outcomes, clinical practice expectations and students’ level of learning. 
5. Assist in the delivery of a three-hour primary skills lab for students, in collaboration with faculty (Tuesday September 8, 2026 – times to be confirmed).
6. Collaborate with faculty in facilitating an in-person four hours community simulation on Monday, November 2nd, 2026 (times to be confirmed). 
7. Encourage an inquiring, theory, and evidence-based approach to nursing practice.
8. Facilitate interprofessional and intraprofessional communication among students and all members of the health care team.
9. Collaborate with the NUR461 course instructors to plan the weekly virtual clinical discussion sessions in alignment with the course theoretical concepts. 
10. Prepare for and facilitate a weekly online, synchronous two-hour clinical discussion with the students, reflecting on virtual case scenarios completed by the students and building on existing clinical experiences. 
11. Monitor student performance and assess student progress with meeting the clinical practice expectations for the course.  
12. Participate in weekly email communication with Course Faculty and provide a brief update and / or identify any concerns about students’ experiences and clinical progress. 
13. Ensure ongoing communication with students, preceptors, and course faculty. 
14. Provide students with ongoing verbal feedback about their progress in meeting the clinical expectations for the course.
15. Collaborate with course faculty throughout the term as needed to deal with student clinical placement issues.
16. Complete an online written final clinical evaluation of each student incorporating feedback from all clinical shifts that highlights their learning and progress in meeting the clinical practice expectations for the community placement.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
Qualifications:
1. Excellent understanding of primary health care principles and the social determinants of health. 
2. Professional nursing experience in public health and community health, including rural and/or underserved settings.
3. Current registration with the College of Nurses of Ontario as a Registered Nurse or Registered Nurse in the Extended Class. 
4. Current membership in the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario (RNAO) is preferred. 
5. Master's degree in Nursing or equivalent is preferred; BScN is required. 
6. Previous teaching experience will be considered an asset.                                                                                                                7. Excellent facilitation, analytical, and written and oral communication skills.                                                                                                                 8. Competent in MS Office applications.                             9. Experience using online Learning Management Systems.
Note to Graduate Students:
If you are a graduate student and you accept the position, you will agree to the following:
“I confirm that I will be registered as a University of Toronto student or PDF on the date that this appointment begins. I understand that if I should cease to be registered as a University of Toronto student or PDF during the period of this appointment, for any reason other than convocation, I must immediately notify my supervisor, and my appointment may be terminated.”

o Providing high-quality, rubric-based feedback on written and analytical work.
o Working effectively in a collaborative and coordinated teaching team in large courses (> 300 students).

job_title: Methods in Arts Based Health Research
application_procedure: Apply at https://www.utsc.utoronto.ca/webapps/cupehiring/dept/chs/app/ta
duties:
Duties for the position vary and will include:
• Preparing for and supporting classes
• Leading or assisting with tutorials or other forms of classroom instruction
• Holding office hours in person and/or online
• Grading assignments, tests, and examinations in accordance with provided rubrics
• Invigilating examinations
• Providing timely responses to student inquiries
• Supporting course administration using Quercus
• Attending team meetings and training sessions as required
Specific duties are defined in the Description of Duties and Allocation of Hours form by the instructor in charge of the course. Please note duties are performed in person at the UTSC campus.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
Applicants must demonstrate the following:

• Strong knowledge of the subject matter
• Academic qualifications:
Applicants must (a) hold a Bachelor's degree in a health-related discipline and (b) be currently enrolled in a graduate-level (Master's or Doctoral) program in a health-related discipline. Strong academic preparation in health studies is required, including appropriate background knowledge in the relevant subject or specialty.
• Ability to:
o Provide clear, constructive, and timely feedback that supports student learning and development
o Communicate professionally and responsively with instructors, teaching team members, and students
o Uphold a professional standard of conduct befitting an academic environment, including punctuality, reliable attendance, and a demeanor that models expectations for students
o Maintain accuracy and consistency when grading and handling course materials
o Manage time effectively and meet strict deadlines in a high-volume academic environment
• Technical proficiency:
o Quercus or equivalent learning management system
o Microsoft Office Suite: Word, Excel, PowerPoint
• Other requirements:
o Excellent written and oral communication skills in English
o Strong attention to detail and organizational skills
o Ability to uphold academic integrity and confidentiality
qualifications_preferred:
Preference may be given to applicants who demonstrate:
• Advanced knowledge of:
o The conceptual foundations of arts-based health research (ABHR), including its theoretical underpinnings and the range of methods used to generate, interpret, and represent health-related knowledge (e.g., narrative inquiry, performance, visual arts, digital storytelling, body mapping); and key ethical, methodological, and implementation considerations for ABHR in Canadian and global contexts.
• Academic qualifications:
o Multiple courses completed as an undergraduate or graduate student in the subject area; previously taken this course or a similar course
o Graduate-level training or specialization in arts-based research, health humanities, qualitative health research, social sciences, or a closely related interdisciplinary field with a strong methodological component.
• Previous experience in:
o Grading or TA work in qualitative methods, health humanities, or arts-based research courses; experience designing or participating in arts-based research projects; or supporting courses that engage students in experiential and creative forms of learning.
• Demonstrated strengths in:
o Explaining arts-based methodologies and their conceptual foundations clearly to undergraduate students; facilitating discussion of research ethics, evidence, and methodological quality in ABHR; providing constructive feedback on reflective, creative, and analytical written work.
• Other assets:
o Hands-on experience with at least one arts-based research method; familiarity with research ethics processes for creative or community-based health research in Canada.

duties:
Develop and deliver 4-hours of lectures to cover Antipsychotic Pharmacology. (Enrolment ~ 130 students).  Develop and integrate exam/assessment questions for PCL470 and PCL ACP and answer keys. Attend and grade student presentations on neuropharmacology and grade according to grading key. Assist with term test invigilation and term test take up with the PCL470 students. Be familiar with the Quercus learning management system. Work as part of a teaching team, including faculty and teaching assistants. Interact with Dr. Ali Salahpour, course coordinator and program chair/coordinator as needed.
•	2 lectures on antipsychotics (24 hrs). 4h lecture, 20h prep (10h prep/lecture as its new lecture.)
•	Small groups sessions (2 hrs)
•	Term test development (2 hrs)
•	Term test review (2 hrs)
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.

The candidate should have experience in course management (Quercus preferred) and course delivery, managing and mentoring teaching assistants, interacting and mentoring undergraduate students.
qualifications_preferred: Previous experience as a TA or experience in assessment development and grading is an asset.

Please note this course is partnered with the Writing-Integrated Teaching (WIT) program. The WIT program helps instructors embed discipline-specific writing instruction into undergraduate courses to foster students’ writing development and success. As part of your TA duties, ~30 of your hours will be focused on supporting students’ writing. duties:
May include student contact, marking/grading, other duties as required.

Please note this course is partnered with the Writing-Integrated Teaching (WIT) program. The WIT program helps instructors embed discipline-specific writing instruction into undergraduate courses to foster students’ writing development and success. As part of your TA duties, ~30 of your hours will be focused on supporting students’ writing. These tasks include receiving training on WIT-related topics (e.g., the importance of low-stakes writing, best practices for providing feedback on writing), attending benchmarking training sessions to focus on how to evaluate student assignments efficiently and fairly, holding designated office hours for writing assignments, and preparing/delivering writing instruction and resources during office hours.

application_procedure: If interested, please reply to: jobsmcs.utm@utoronto.ca  with the following information by 24 July 2026: 1) your current CV, including a clear description of your teaching experience; 2) your current status; your department; your research area; 3) the name and e-mail address of at least one reference who can comment on your teaching ability (optional).
duties: Duties include: planning the lectures, assignments and tests and marking schemes; providing appropriate contact time outside of class to students through office hours, emails, etc.; invigilating the midterm and final exam; managing the grades and submitting the final course grades; handling student petitions; setting and grading a make-up exam if required. While there is a lot of room for creativity in course delivery, instructors will be expected to follow the basic content and style used by the faculty members who normally teach the course.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than past teaching experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Enrollment in or completion of a graduate degree in Computer Science or Education. Advanced comprehension of the subject matter as evidenced by research activity, industry experience, or teaching experience. Strong organizational, interpersonal, and communication skills.
qualifications_preferred: n/a
ta_support: 54 hours per 30 students enrolled per semester

application_procedure:
Only applications submitted to the email address listed below will be considered. Please include the closing date of the posting in your email subject line.

Applicants should submit the following documents by July 27, 2026  
1. A cover letter outlining suitability for the position (normally 1-2 pages)
2. An updated curriculum vitae
3. Sample instructional materials (maximum 15 pages). E.g.,  handouts, activities, assignment instructions, presentation slides, multi-modal curricula, and course outlines.

5. Names and contact information for three referees

Applications must be submitted by email to the following:
Dr. Marci Prescott-Brown, Director
New College Writing Centre
c/o Nancy Dragicevic, Program Support Officer
newcollege.wcpositions@utoronto.ca
duties: Lead the QT—Write Ins, a group open to the New College 2SLGBTQ+ community and allies and one of the New College Writing Centre’s (NCWC) inclusive spaces. This group provides writing welcome for students as they create their writing assignments and personal creative projects during the Fall and Winter terms. This position will be at least 100 hours; administrative duties as well as teaching will be important in this role. The successful applicant may also teach in the one-on-one program in the NCWC and may also create writing workshops. Applicants should be prepared to teach both in-person and online.  The person in this role will liaise with various student services in the New College context to deliver innovative programing.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
Applicants must possess a completed Masters degree, familiarity with queer scholarship/theory, and demonstrated community building and advocacy in the 2SLGBTQ+ community. They must have an active registration in a program in the School of Graduate Studies at the University of Toronto. Demonstrated ability to teach writing and communication skills to students from diverse cultural and linguistic backgrounds. 
Previous experience teaching writing at the university level, either in a university writing centre and/or in university level courses with significant writing components. Excellent written and oral communication skills. Demonstrated ability to teach academic writing and critical skills in a one-to-one mode and across a variety of disciplines and programs, including those of New College. Demonstrated ability to facilitate interactive workshops. Familiarity with forms of writing and oral communication in a broad range of disciplinary curricula. Superior ability to work closely with the genre, rhetoric and grammar of student texts. The ability to work cooperatively and effectively in a highly collaborative environment.
qualifications_preferred:
Lived experience as a member of the 2SLGBTQ+ community
Prior experience facilitating writing opportunities in the 2SLGBTQ+ community 
A completed PhD or completed PhD coursework

Regular TA Postings - All applicants registered in our Economics graduate programs (MA, MFE, PHD) must submit information via an electronic link sent to their UofT email address by the TA Coordinator.  3. Regular TA Postings - Non-Economics graduate and undergraduate student applicants must submit an application to the Economics TA Coordinator using the application link on https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/graduate/taOpenings, including specifying which TA position(s) you are applying for; your UtorID, providing your U of T (only) email address; and your current program of study.  4. NOTE: For all Emergency TA Postings all applicants submit application information to the Economics TA Coordinator (the URL link for the application form is provided in the emergency job posting), specifying which TA position(s) you are applying for; your UtorID, providing your U of T (only) email address; and your current program of study. 5. UG course descriptions can be found at https://artsci.calendar.utoronto.ca/section/Economics and https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/undergraduate/courses/2

See TA job postings on the department website at https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/graduate/taOpenings and CUPE3902Unit1 postings at http://unit1.hrandequity.utoronto.ca/. 2. Regular TA Postings - All applicants registered in our Economics graduate programs (MA, MFE, PHD) must submit information via an electronic link sent to their UofT email address by the TA Coordinator.  3. Regular TA Postings - Non-Economics graduate and undergraduate student applicants must submit an application to the Economics TA Coordinator using the application link on https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/graduate/taOpenings, including specifying which TA position(s) you are applying for; your UtorID, providing your U of T (only) email address; and your current program of study.  4. NOTE: For all Emergency TA Postings all applicants submit application information to the Economics TA Coordinator (the URL link for the application form is provided in the emergency job posting), specifying which TA position(s) you are applying for; your UtorID, providing your U of T (only) email address; and your current program of study. 5. UG course descriptions can be found at https://artsci.calendar.utoronto.ca/section/Economics and https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/undergraduate/courses/2
duties: Duties will be arranged by the instructor and may include grading tests, exams, and papers, holding office hours, holding tutorials, invigilating tests and exams, and other duties related to the course. Duties may have an online component. Completion of all grading of all student work that is completed during the term of the appointment, up to and including final exams is expected.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Academic qualifications, experience in invigilation, grading and tutoring in a similar course
qualifications_preferred: Graduate-level knowledge of Economics
salery: $53.92/hr (+4% vacation pay). Please note that should rates stipulated in the collective agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the collective agreement shall prevail.

Regular TA Postings - All applicants registered in our Economics graduate programs (MA, MFE, PHD) must submit information via an electronic link sent to their UofT email address by the TA Coordinator.  3. Regular TA Postings - Non-Economics graduate and undergraduate student applicants must submit an application to the Economics TA Coordinator using the application link on https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/graduate/taOpenings, including specifying which TA position(s) you are applying for; your UtorID, providing your U of T (only) email address; and your current program of study.  4. NOTE: For all Emergency TA Postings all applicants submit application information to the Economics TA Coordinator (the URL link for the application form is provided in the emergency job posting), specifying which TA position(s) you are applying for; your UtorID, providing your U of T (only) email address; and your current program of study. 5. UG course descriptions can be found at https://artsci.calendar.utoronto.ca/section/Economics and https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/undergraduate/courses/2
duties: Duties will be arranged by the instructor and may include grading tests, exams, and papers, holding office hours, holding tutorials, invigilating tests and exams, and other duties related to the course. Duties may have an online component. Completion of all grading of all student work that is completed during the term of the appointment, up to and including final exams is expected.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Academic qualifications, experience in invigilation, grading and tutoring in a similar course
qualifications_preferred: Graduate-level knowledge of Economics
salery: $53.92/hr (+4% vacation pay). Please note that should rates stipulated in the collective agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the collective agreement shall prevail.

duties: The Lead Writing TA (LWTA) will work with WIT staff and the WIT Unit Contact to support course instructors and TAs in selected courses, as well as support the embedding of undergraduate student writing development in the hiring department. The LWTA will perform the following duties: Participate in mandatory training sessions throughout the year; Read book chapters, articles, etc. on writing education pedagogy; Meet with WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts, and course instructors to plan writing assignments activities and for tutorials, labs, or lectures; Design and lead training sessions and consultations for course TAs on writing-related topics, such as providing feedback to students on critical reading, writing, planning tutorial/lab activities related to writing, etc.; Develop and/or adapt resources on writing instruction (such as marking rubrics and assignments) for instructors, TAs, and students; Communicate regularly by email and in person with course instructors, WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts and others to implement WIT activities; Collaborate with LWTAs in other units by meeting in peer groups and sharing writing instruction resources; Collect and analyze data on WIT (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, TA and instructor focus groups, etc.); Document activities (e.g., help draft unit WIT goals, provide monthly updates, write a final a report about WIT activities in the unit, etc.)

job_title: Interdisciplinary Special Topics - The Happy Mind: Scientific Perspectives on Wellness
application_procedure: Please submit applications via the Arts and Science TA System: https://taships.iit.artsci.utoronto.ca/new/postings
duties:
NEW271H1F  – Explore the science of how to cultivate a Happy Mind. This course will merge cutting-edge research from cognitive neuroscience, psychology, and contemplative traditions to investigate what it means to live a fulfilling life. Students will engage with scientific models of well-being, including computational neuroscience models, computational psychiatry, and the neuroscience of emotions. They will study how the mind constructs its reality through predictions, emotions, and habits, examining both the promises and perils of these processes.

DUTIES: Holding office hours; consulting with the course instructors; advising students about and marking assignments; and attending in person lectures.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: The candidate should be familiar with topics and content addressed in the course, particularly in relation to models and theories related to mindfulness, meditation, cognitive neuroscience and wellbeing.
qualifications_preferred: Preference will be given to applicants with an understanding of, or engaged in research related to, mindfulness meditation, and/or contemplative practices.
tutorial: Lectures: Wednesdays 10:00 AM - 1:00 PM [Note: the delivery mode is currently In Person and the successful applicant will need to be available to attend lectures].

job_title: Interdisciplinary Special Topics- The Architecture of Wellness: Braiding Indigenous & Buddhist Teachings
application_procedure: Please submit applications via the Arts and Science TA System: https://taships.iit.artsci.utoronto.ca/new/postings
duties:
NEW372H1S  – This course draws upon the knowledge streams of Indigenous and Buddhist perspectives to explore wellness on the individual and system levels. Classes take place both in a classroom and the Mother Earth Learning lodge at New College. Teachings focus on relationality, interconnection to the earth, along conceptual models of attention, self and non-self. Investigate questions related to building inclusive communities amidst an increasingly divisive and complex world. The course integrates multi-modal learning modalities, including didactic lectures, hands-on experiential practices and land-based learning.

DUTIES: Holding office hours; consulting with the course instructors; advising students about and marking assignments; and attending in person lectures.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: The candidate should be familiar with topics and content addressed in the course, particularly in relation to the practices and theories of meditation and mindfulness.
qualifications_preferred: Preference will be given to applicants with an understanding of, or engaged in research related to, Buddhist Studies, Indigenous Studies, and/or the interactions between Buddhism and Psychology.
tutorial: Lectures: 1:00 PM- 4:00 PM [Note: the delivery mode is currently In Person and the successful applicant will need to be available to attend lectures].

job_title: Introduction to Critical Equity and Solidarity Studies
department: New College - Critical Studies in Equity and Solidarity
application_procedure: Please submit applications via the Arts and Science TA System: https://taships.iit.artsci.utoronto.ca/new/postings
duties:
CSE240H1 – An interdisciplinary intersectional interrogation and examination of systemic inequity, structural oppression and social justice in local and global contexts. Provides a foundation for studies in critical equity and solidarity through a concentrated focus on theory and practice as it relates to major concepts, historical perspectives, key debates, lived experience and radical grassroots community resistance to inequity and oppression. Introduces critical equity as a theoretical framework through which to approach social relations of race, ethnicity, class, gender, sexuality and disability and as a site for thinking through formations of solidarity and transformative social change. The concept of self-defense articulated historically by the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense among others is a key component.

job_title: Abolition in the Global Context: Theorizing Uprisings and Youth Activism against Policing and Prisons
department: New College - Critical Studies in Equity and Solidarity
application_procedure:
Applicants should submit — in one consolidated application package (either PDF or word document) — a detailed letter of application; an updated curriculum vitae; names and contact information (email and phone) for two referees or two reference letters; evidence of teaching in the relevant area, including student evaluations if available by July 17, 2026. 

Applications should be sent to the New College Program Administrator.

Email: nc.hiring@utoronto.ca. 

Application materials must be submitted via email and not through the University of Toronto job board. Late or incomplete applications will not be considered.
duties:
CSE340H1 –Considers the question: what does abolition mean in a global context? An analysis of how nation-states use prisons, (im)migrant detention centers, black sites, detention camps, military prisons, border checkpoints, refugee camps, walls, and concentration camps, to surveil, contain, and lock up disposable populations, and/or to suppress those that resist state violence. Explores these carceral spaces through a historical and political economic investigation of the processes that have produced these sites. Draws on anti-carceral perspectives on abolition and reform to examine uprisings and political activism, particularly youth activism, against prisons, policing, and forms of militarized, capitalist violence transnationally.

DUTIES: Normal duties related to the design and teaching of a university credit course, including preparation and delivery of course content; development, administration and marking of assignments, tests and exams; calculation and submission of grades; and holding regular office hours.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: PhD in progress required. Academic background in social justice education and familiarity with anti-oppressive pedagogies. The candidate should have a research and/or teaching background in or across the following areas of scholarship: Theories of racial and colonial violence, anti-colonial and anti-imperialist thought and practice, grass-roots organizing for resistance and liberation at the local and global level.
qualifications_preferred: Experience teaching social justice issues in a university setting is preferred.

job_title:
NEW106H1F: Science, Health and Social Justice
NEW116H1S: Science and Global Threats
application_procedure:
Applicants should submit a detailed letter of application; an updated curriculum vitae; names and contact information (email and phone) for two referees or two reference letters; evidence of teaching in the relevant area, including student evaluations if available by July 17, 2026. 

Applications should be sent to the New College Program Administrator.

Email: nc.hiring@utoronto.ca. 

Application materials must be submitted via email and not through the University of Toronto job board. Late or incomplete applications will not be considered.
duties:
NEW106H1F & NEW116H1S are two of ten small interdisciplinary seminars comprising the first-year foundational program, New One: Learning without Borders. The courses reflect the program’s defining theme, “learning without borders,” by exploring a locally relevant issue through a transnational lens, building students’ capacities as critically reflective learners, emphasizing creative pedagogy and interdisciplinarity, engaging the perspectives of community members and senior students; and fostering student development overall.

NEW106H1F: How can scientific knowledge and research be mobilized to impact individual and global health? How is health impacted by social, racial and economic inequalities? This course explores scientific research and practice with special attention to the translation of scientific knowledge in the public sphere, and its ability to inform policies, practices and laws. Students have the opportunity to meet with clinician-scientists, policy-makers, and other professionals connected to the health care system.

NEW116H1S: What is the role of science in addressing current global threats? What are the possibilities and the limitations of scientific research and knowledge in tackling complex problems such as climate change, pandemics and pollution? In this course, students explore these questions by examining case studies, meeting with specialists in various scientific fields, and engaging in research on a topic of their own choice.

New One courses meet for 3 hours each week.  A portion of those sessions will occasionally be allocated to “Learning Labs.”  All students in the program come together in these Learning Labs to engage in joint activities and explore issues that are common to the four topics.   Both in seminars and Learning Labs, students will experience a variety of ways of learning (through, for example, guest lectures, group work, workshops, field trips to local community organizations, and panels of senior students sharing their experience and insights).

Duties
1. Planning and teaching the seminar course, grading and giving feedback on assignments, providing office hours for academic counseling of students
2. Liaising with the program coordinator and other New One instructors to coordinate and design assignments, plan plenary sessions and evaluate the program.   
3. Incorporating guest speakers and field trips into the course with administrative support from the program coordinator and assistant. 
4. Participating in and occasionally leading the activities of the plenary sessions.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: A completed or in-progress Ph.D (or equivalent) with a scholarly background in areas related to the specific course. Teaching experience at the university level and a demonstrated familiarity with related social justice issues is required.
qualifications_preferred: Evidence teaching first-year students in foundation programs. Evidence of an interdisciplinary approach in scholarship as well as community engagement in areas related to the specific course is an asset.

Lectures take place IN-PERSON on Thursdays from 12:00-15:00, with a concurrent online section.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
Ph.D. in Psychology (or a closely related discipline) completed or near completion.

Demonstrated expertise in psychological research methods and design, with a strong emphasis on scientific literacy and critical evaluation of research.

Prior university-level teaching experience in research methods, statistics, or scientific writing in psychology.

Applicants should demonstrate a strong background in the theory and application of research methods used in psychological science, with a proven ability to teach students to become scientifically literate consumers of research.
qualifications_preferred: Experience teaching large courses

The lead teaching assistant will lead and manage the teaching assistants for this large, introductory course so as to ensure that students are supported and engaged.  

Their responsibilities will include ensuring proper and timely communication among the course instructors, teaching assistants, and students. The lead teaching assistant will help devise tutorial learning strategies and outlines; run weekly meetings for the teaching assistants at which they address the tutorial strategies and content to help ensure consistency and relevance; provide one-to-one support for teaching assistants as necessary; ensure consistency in grading across tutorials; help manage communications systems and processes with students; share key course announcements from the course instructor with the class; organize and assist with invigilation of tests and exams; and other duties as assigned by the instructor.  

This is a leadership position that requires problem solving and a thoughtful approach to working with peers and students.

job_title: Coping with Complexity in Persistent Illness (Adult)
application_procedure: "Please click on the "How to Apply" tab on our website: https://bloomberg.nursing.utoronto.ca/careers/clinical-instructor-positions
duties:
The Lawrence Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing Program provides a full-time, two-year, second entry baccalaureate nursing education. Given that nursing is a practice-based profession, we believe that clinical experience is a vital and integral component of the curriculum, providing students with hands-on opportunities to actively apply classroom learning to ‘real life’ nursing practice under the supervision and support of a clinical teacher. Clinical Instructors (CIs) play an important role in facilitating student learning and providing excellent clinical learning experiences to further develop students’ knowledge, skill, and judgement. 
	
CIs should be flexible and prepared to utilize additional learning modalities such as simulation and virtual clinical learning in the event a clinical day is cancelled by the organization. CIs will work closely with the Clinical Education Office and Course Faculty to develop and share resources to support the continuity of clinical teaching and student learning. CIs are required to complete all on-boarding and orientation activities required by the faculty as well as the clinical placement site prior to the start of the placement in preparation for their clinical teaching role. Ongoing support will be provided throughout the term by Course Faculty, the Undergraduate Clinical Resource Faculty, and the Clinical Education Office.  Applicants should note that commitment to the role for the entire term is needed for consistency in the development, assessment, and evaluation of nursing students.


Duties and Responsibilities:   
1.	Complete the asynchronous CI orientation learning modules as required and attend the in-person Faculty-led CI orientation for NUR460.
2.	Complete all on-boarding and orientation required by the University as well as the clinical placement site prior to the first clinical shift.
3.	Familiarize themselves with and uphold all faculty and University policies related to clinical placements (e.g., CI and Student Absences, Professional Behaviour). 
4.	Attend all clinical shifts as scheduled. 
4A.	Personal obligations (e.g. attending conferences, vacations, or appointments) are to be scheduled outside of the clinical shifts. 
4B.	In the case of extenuating circumstances (e.g. illness and/or bereavement) - CIs are required to notify the Clinical Education Office with the dates as soon as possible. CIs may also be asked to assist the Clinical Education Office to arrange a possible replacement instructor. 
5.	Prepare patient assignments prior to students' arrival on the nursing unit, reflective of the students' level of expertise.
6.	Assist with and assess the students' preparation for their clinical activities with attention to integration of nursing, pathophysiological, pharmacologic, and therapeutic concepts.
6A.	Note: New for the 26-27 academic year – students to be enrolled in NUR440 (Foundations of Mental Health and Substance Use) concurrent with NUR460.
6B.	Students are expected to apply learning from all courses during their clinical shifts through the provision of nursing care, as well as by engaging in active discussions with the clinical instructor (CI) and the clinical group.
7.	Encourage an inquiring, theory, and evidence-based approach to nursing practice.
8.	Facilitate interprofessional and intraprofessional communication among students and all members of the health care team.
9.	Supervise and assist students with providing nursing care while monitoring and assessing students’ progress with meeting clinical practice expectations.
10.	Collaborate with nursing staff and other health care providers in gaining assistance and access to learning experiences for the students.
11.	Foster reflection and discussion about clinical learning among students during post clinical conferences
12.	Participate in weekly email communication with Course Faculty and provide a brief update and/or identify any concerns about students’ experiences and clinical progress. 
13.	Provide students with ongoing verbal feedback about their progress meeting the clinical practice expectations for the course. 
14.	Provide written midterm and online written final clinical evaluations of each student that highlights their learning and progress with meeting the clinical practice expectations for the clinical placement.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
"Qualifications:
1. Interest in clinical teaching and working with novice nursing students.
2. Minimum of 2-3 years of current and relevant clinical experience (adult medical-surgical or adult mental health nursing experience). 
3. Current registration with the College of Nurses of Ontario as a Registered Nurse or Registered Nurse in the Extended Class. 
4. BScN required; MN will be considered an asset.
5. Previous experience in clinical instructing or as a preceptor is an asset.

Note to Graduate Students:
If you are a graduate student and you accept the position, you will agree to the following:
“I confirm that I will be registered as a University of Toronto student or PDF on the date that this appointment begins. I understand that if I should cease to be registered as a University of Toronto student or PDF during the period of this appointment, for any reason other than convocation, I must immediately notify my supervisor, and my appointment may be terminated.”

Applicants should note that commitment to the role for each scheduled shift for the entire term is needed for consistency in the development, assessment, and evaluation of nursing students.

Duties and Responsibilities:	
1.	Complete the asynchronous CI orientation learning modules as required and attend the in-person Faculty-led CI orientation for NUR365.
2.	Complete all on-boarding and orientation required by the University as well as the clinical placement site prior to the first clinical shift.
3.	Familiarize themselves with and uphold all faculty and University policies related to clinical placements (e.g., CI and Student Absences, Professional Behaviour).

a.	Personal obligations (e.g. attending conferences, vacations, or appointments) are to be scheduled outside of the clinical shifts.

a.	Note: Students are expected to apply learning from the classroom and lab settings each week during their clinical shifts. application_procedure: "Please click on the "How to Apply" tab on our website: https://bloomberg.nursing.utoronto.ca/careers/clinical-instructor-positions
duties:
The Lawrence Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing Program provides a full-time, two-year, second entry baccalaureate nursing education. Given that nursing is a practice-based profession, we believe that clinical experience is a vital and integral component of the curriculum, providing students with hands-on opportunities to actively apply classroom learning to ‘real life’ nursing practice under the supervision and support of a clinical teacher. Clinical Instructors (CIs) play an important role in facilitating student learning and providing excellent clinical learning experiences to further develop students’ knowledge, skill, and judgement. 
	
CIs should be flexible and prepared to utilize additional learning modalities such as simulation and virtual clinical learning in the event a clinical day is cancelled by the organization. CIs will work closely with the Clinical Education Office and Course Faculty to develop and share resources to support the continuity of clinical teaching and student learning. CIs are required to complete all on-boarding and orientation activities required by the faculty as well as the clinical placement site prior to the start of the placement in preparation for their clinical teaching role. Ongoing support will be provided throughout the term by Course Faculty, the Undergraduate Clinical Resource Faculty, and the Clinical Education Office. Applicants should note that commitment to the role for each scheduled shift for the entire term is needed for consistency in the development, assessment, and evaluation of nursing students.

Duties and Responsibilities:	
1.	Complete the asynchronous CI orientation learning modules as required and attend the in-person Faculty-led CI orientation for NUR365.
2.	Complete all on-boarding and orientation required by the University as well as the clinical placement site prior to the first clinical shift.
3.	Familiarize themselves with and uphold all faculty and University policies related to clinical placements (e.g., CI and Student Absences, Professional Behaviour). 
4.	Attend all clinical shifts as scheduled. 
a.	Personal obligations (e.g. attending conferences, vacations, or appointments) are to be scheduled outside of the clinical shifts. 
b.	In the case of extenuating circumstances (e.g. illness and/or bereavement) - CIs are required to notify the Clinical Education Office with the dates as soon as possible. CIs may also be asked to assist the Clinical Education Office to arrange a possible replacement instructor. 
5.	Prepare patient assignments prior to students’ arrival on the nursing unit, reflective of the students’ level of expertise.
6.	Facilitate students’ application of theory related to foundational therapeutic, relational, and health assessment skills.
a.	Note: Students are expected to apply learning from the classroom and lab settings each week during their clinical shifts. This is done through practising the learned assessments and skills as part of the nursing care provided, as well as engaging in active discussions about the learning with the CI and clinical group. 
7.	Assist with and assess the students’ preparation for their clinical activities with attention to integration of nursing, pathophysiological, and therapeutic concepts.
8.	Encourage an inquiring, theory, and evidence-informed approach to nursing practice.
9.	Facilitate interprofessional and intraprofessional communication and collaboration between students and all members of the health care team.
10.	Directly supervise and assist students as they relate to and provide care for older persons, while also monitoring and assessing students’ progress with meeting the clinical practice expectations. 
11.	Collaborate with nursing staff in gaining assistance and access to learning experiences for the students.
12.	Foster reflection and critical discussion about clinical learning among students during weekly post-clinical conferences.
13.	Participate in weekly email communication with Course Faculty and provide a brief update and/or identify any concerns about students’ experiences and clinical progress. 
14.	Provide students with ongoing verbal feedback about their progress with meeting the clinical practice expectations for the course. 
15.	Provide written midterm and final online evaluations incorporating feedback from all clinical shifts.
16.	Arrange and facilitate a short 1:1 virtual meeting to review each students’ final evaluation within a week of the final clinical shift.

duties:
As part of the English Language Learning (ELL) Initiative in Arts and Science, the Lead TA will work with the ELL Coordinators to support Course Instructors and train regular course SIAs in ACT371H1. The LTA will perform the following duties:

o	Regular 1:1 training and collaboration with ELL coordinator or other ELL faculty
o	Participate in cross-disciplinary lunch and discussion sessions for Lead TAs
o	Meet with ELL coordinators and course instructors to plan course assignments and tutorials
o	Design and lead training sessions for course TAs on topics such as marking assignments and presentations, as well as planning tutorial activities integrating ACT371H1 course content to scholarly reading, professional writing, oral presentation, discussion skills, and intercultural communication
o	Develop and/or adapt existing resources on reading, oral presentation, and writing instruction for course instructors, TAs, and students
o	Communicate regularly with course instructors, ELL coordinators, and others to coordinate activities
o	Collect and summarize data (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, etc.)

Duties of this position shall be performed on the St. George Campus.

•	Regular 1:1 training and collaboration with ELL coordinator or other ELL faculty

•	Design and lead workshops for students on topics including professional writing, scholarly reading, and oral presentation
•	Collect and summarize data (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, etc.)

duties:
Teaching assistants will prepare motivating and stimulating materials that engage students in dynamic and interactive learning during practicums, provide constructive feedback and encourage confidence-building participation that develops students’ competence in academic oral and written communication. The focus is on critical thinking, reading, and writing skills. Students will engage actively in collaborative peer learning through consistent engagement with study group interactions, Peer Scholar interactions, assigned group work outside class as well as through online learning.

Responsible for leading one or two in-person practicum sessions per week on Wednesdays, 11am - 12pm and/or 12pm – 1 pm in a way that establishes a robust highly-interactive learning community in the physical class; conduct office hours; grade projects, assignments, oral presentations and reflective journals, mid-terms and final assessments in a timely manner; facilitate the discussion board to stimulate an active and genuine exchange of ideas and deepening of critical thinking skills; grade quality of participation in discussion board and Peer Scholar; responsible for conducting assessments in accordance with criteria specified and provide supportive written feedback and guidance that helps individual students to develop their vocabulary and increase their critical thinking and academic writing skills.

Appointment Dates: September 8, 2026 – December 22, 2026
Practicum Dates: September 16, 2026 - November 11, 2026
Practicum Times: Wednesdays, 11am- 12pm and/or 12:00-1:00pm (In-person)
Office Hours: To be arranged

Apply here: https://www.utsc.utoronto.ca/webapps/cupehiring/dept/ctl/app/ta

To complete the application, please upload the following documents:
1. Cover Letter
2. Curriculum Vitae or Resume
3. A short writing sample (maximum 4 pages)
4. Contact information of THREE references.
5. Teaching evaluations (optional)
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
A minimum Master’s degree in a relevant field; a minimum of 3 years of experience teaching at a university; evidence of ability to support the development of critical thinking and academic communication skills (especially academic writing) of multilingual university students who have significant needs related to English for Academic Purposes; relevant experience teaching writing one-on-one and/or teaching in university courses with a significant writing instruction component; demonstrated ability and high level of comfort discussing and teaching grammar to students in university courses; excellent communication and motivational skills, demonstrated ability to be perceived as highly approachable by students; and demonstrated ability to create a lively, highly motivating learning environment and sustain students’ interest in learning (as reflected through student attendance and engagement throughout the semester).

Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_preferred: Experience supporting student learning through an online environment; training in writing, education, or applied linguistics would be a great asset; experience working with a diverse group of students and training in second-language instruction at the university level; demonstrated evidence of active involvement in research in the field of second language writing; a demonstrated ability and experience using Canvas (Quercus), and knowledge and experience using web conferencing tools such as Microsoft Teams and Zoom.

duties:
Attend class (Monday 11-12): introduction lecture and preparation experiments Attend class (Tuesday 9-12): Supervise students in the lab; teach techniques; actively monitor students’ performance with constructive criticism, where appropriate, to help them improve their skills; at the end of each lab, check to see that all materials are incubated or stored in the appropriate place; mark students' reports and performance. Attend weekly lab meeting Mark lab reports .

A laboratory course in bacteriology. Students will perform a variety of genetic and biochemical experiments to identify and characterize unknown organisms and learn how bacteria live together in biofilm and become resistant to antibiotics. Students will also learn important research tools and concepts including CRISPRi, transduction, and conjugation through experiments. Valuable not only for advanced work in microbiology but also in related fields that make use of bacteria and bacteriophages as research tools
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.

application_procedure: Applicants must submit a cover letter, C.V. or resume, and a list of references by July 15, 2026. Applications should be sent via email to the Robert Gillespie Academic Skills Centre at academicskills.utm@utoronto.ca with the subject title: PELS Unit 1 Job Posting.
duties: As a Teaching Assistant for the Robert Gillespie Academic Skills Centre’s (RGASC) Professional English Language Skills (PELS), the TA will perform the following duties: Monitoring PELS discussion boards, Facilitating participation in student hours and events (in person), and Marking assessments using answer keys
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Applicants must be currently enrolled as a Master or PhD graduate student at the University of Toronto. Applicants should have at least two years of experience as a Teaching Assistant at the University of Toronto. Applicants should have experience and facility with administering Quercus sites. Applicants must have the ability to work effectively with and show tact when communicating with PELS administrators, students, and faculty.
qualifications_preferred: Preference will be given to a student who has completed PELS or is interested in helping students with academic English language learning and study skills development and has an interest in intercultural communication strategies and linguistic diversity. An academic background in writing and communication or experience in writing instruction (as a student learner or teaching assistant) is an asset but not required – training will be provided.

job_title: GPD Career Course: Skills for Regulatory Affairs
application_procedure: Applicants should submit (by email) a cover letter, CV, and one letter of reference. Applications should be sent my email and addressed to, Attn: Sobiga Vyravanathan, Institute of Medical Science, University of Toronto, C. David Naylor Building, 6 Queen's Park Crescent, Suite 119, Toronto, Ontario, M5S 3H2, Email: cc.medscience@utoronto.ca
duties: Preparation of course content; Teaching a two-hour class, Wednesdays 4-6 pm; Development, administration, and marking of assignments; calculation and submission of grades; holding regular office hours
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than past teaching experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: The successful candidate is required to have a graduate degree in biomedical science or related discipline. The successful candidate is required to have expertise in in vitro diagnostics across the full product lifecycle, from development through post-market activities and expertise in regulatory strategy development, including balancing time to market with the achievement of optimal labeling claims. The successful candidate must have global regulatory experience, including preparation and submission of pre-market registrations in key jurisdictions (Canada, United States, European Union). The successful candidate must have regulatory affairs experience, ideally including leadership or managerial responsibilities. The successful candidate should have contributed to the development of international regulatory guidance and standards. The successful candidate should have evidence of scholarly contribution to Regulatory Science, including peer-reviewed publications and invited presentations at regulatory conferences. The successful candidate should have experience in curriculum development.
qualifications_preferred: Not applicable
salery: $4721.49 (plus vacation pay). If this is the first CI appointment: $5276.58 (plus vacation pay)

job_title: Aids, Outbreaks, Pandemics: Challenges and Successes in Africa
application_procedure:
Applicants should submit a cover letter, C.V. (including previous teaching evaluations (if applicable)), and a list of references by July 08, 2026. 

Applications should be sent to: African Studies Business Officer
E-mail: businessofficer.afr@utoronto.ca

Please be advised late or incomplete applications will not be accepted.
duties:
JAH350H1: Explores a range of historical and current outbreaks, pandemics and epidemics such as AIDS, COVID-19 and Ebola in Africa through a social science lens. It introduces students to major concepts, events and debates that have and continue to shape understanding of the HIV/AIDS epidemic, Ebola outbreak and COVID-19 pandemic, and the challenges and responses to these distinct health crises. Through a critical lens, students will engage diverse and interdisciplinary scholarship from public health, medical anthropology, sociology, African studies, and related fields within the health and social sciences fields to explore the converging factors that influence responses to health crises and their broader implications.

DUTIES:	 Holding office hours; consulting with and meeting the course instructor; advising students about and marking assignments; and may include attending lectures.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
Current PhD or MA/MPH student in any discipline with a scholarly background in African Studies, Public Health and areas related to specific course required. Preference will be given to applicants engaged in related research.  Teaching experience required.
Additionally, a background in Human Biology, African Studies, and/or Public Health preferred.
qualifications_preferred: Teaching, facilitation and presentation skills and/or experience leading tutorials preferred. Relevant teaching experience at the university-level preferred, with at least one year’s experience as a course TA, whether leading tutorials/labs, in-class instruction, or grading.
tutorial: Note: the course delivery mode is currently in-person.

The application package for each course must include a single file in PDF format, containing Unit 1 completed application form available here, https://www.utm.utoronto.ca/iccit/media/1127/download?inline, cover letter, and resume. Please send your package to ICCIT Director email, ccitjobs@utoronto.ca.
duties: (Unless specified as marking positions may include but are not limited to one or more of the following): Conducting in-person tutorials and maintaining scheduled office hours, students e-mail contact, grading, invigilating, and clerical duties. The mode of instruction for the courses is in-person. Candidates must travel to UTM to perform duties.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than previous experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Candidates must be enrolled in a graduate degree program at the University of Toronto and have equivalent experience specific to the subject matter. Must be familiar with Quercus. For marking positions, candidates must be able to work well under time constraints and meet deadlines.
qualifications_preferred: Background in research and writing is required. Teaching and managing groups of students would be an asset. Editing student work.

duties:
AFR280Y1: Introduction to grammar and basic vocabulary of Swahili. Emphasis on comprehension and oral practice. Reading of selected texts. Relation of the language to its East African cultural context.

Relevant teaching experience at the university-level preferred, with at least one year’s experience as a course TA, whether leading tutorials/labs, in-class instruction, or grading.
tutorial: Note: the course delivery mode is currently online synchronous.

application_procedure:

duties: Preparation and delivery of course materials (including but not limited to lectures, practicals, assignments and tests), preparation and delivery of assignments, tests and/or exams; supervision of teaching assistants (includes providing instruction on how to mark, as well as holding office hours online); marking of students’ work (during the term and beyond if an extension of time is granted by the university officials); submission of grades to university officials; and holding regular office hours.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: The successful applicant should be in a PhD program in Anthropology with a focus on palaeoanthropology, fossilized hominin remains, primatology, evolutionary principles and/or other aspects of this course as described.
qualifications_preferred: Experience with management and delivery of courses using University of Toronto or similar online systems (e.g., Quercus, Canvas-based technologies, Zoom, etc.) is preferred. Evidence of successful teaching in relevant courses and previous TA experience in ANT203, ANT332 and/or ANT333, are assets.
salery: $9,442.98. Please note that should rates stipulated in the collective agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the collective agreement shall prevail.
ta_support: Up to 640 hours (total number of assigned hours is dependent on course enrolment and course approvals)
tutorial: Delivery mode: In person. See https://ttb.utoronto.ca/ for timetable of lectures and practicals.

application_procedure: A complete application must include (i) a cover letter that outlines your relevant teaching experience, (ii) your resume, and (iii) 2 references (name and contact information), at least one of which is an instructor you have worked with. These documents must  be assembled in a SINGLE pdf and submitted to  director.engsci@utoronto.ca with the subject line "ESC180 Course Instructor Application" by the specified deadline.  If during the application and/or selection process you require accommodation due to a disability, please contact Diane Giang at director.engsci@utoronto.ca.
duties:
The Division of Engineering Science is looking for an instructor to teach ESC180 H1F.  The instructors will be responsible for:

Preparing and delivering lecture content, ideally incorporating active learning pedagogy; preparing in-laboratory activities to help students to solidify their understanding of course materials; preparing and developing assessment tools for independent or group assignments that integrate across course materials;  preparing and developing assessment tools for closely supervised tests (preparation of marking rubrics, term test/s, final exam); assessing/grading student work.  Hiring and managing Teaching Assistants for the course including mentoring, and providing necessary training as per the CUPE 3902 Unit 1 Memorandum of Agreement. Hold weekly office hour, and attend practical sessions as needed.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than past teaching experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Open to graduate students who have an APSC B.A.Sc. or equivalent. Excellent programming skills in Python and C (with an emphasis on bit manipulation, pointers, and memory management). Thorough and fluent understanding of the Python and C memory models and complexity analysis. Excellent skills in designing recursive functions. Excellent communication skills when talking about programming and algorithms.  Must have served as a Teaching Assistant (or equivalent) or Course Instructor for at least one introductory programming course, ESC180H1 and/or ESC190H1; must be familiar with implementing data structures including linked lists, hash tables, queues, stacks, trees, graphs, heaps, etc., and their analysis; must be familiar with algorithms e.g. sorting, searching, graph traversal, etc., and their analysis; should be familiar with contemporary introductory programming pedagogy.
qualifications_preferred: Engineering Science graduates preferred. Enthusiasm for teaching beginners.

duties:
Responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following:  

Supervising in-person assessments (i.e. Mastery Exercises and Progress Tests) across the MD Program; some remote shifts may be required.

Maintaining confidentiality and security of student and assessment information.

Reporting anomalous situations

Verifying student identification.

Making assessment related announcements to students.

qualifications: Applicants must be currently registered as students pursuing a graduate degree with the School of Graduate Studies at the University of Toronto; Applicants must be available for in-person invigilation shifts between August 24, 2026 and June 30, 2027; Applicants must be available for virtual orientation week of August 17, 2026; Experience invigilating tests/exams is an asset; Understanding and ability to apply relevant University and government policies, procedures and legislation.

duties: Teaching a course titled “Family Practice Across the Life Cycle: Caregiving and Older Adults” based on the approved course outline. Preference may be given to candidates who have the most experience teaching in this area at the graduate level.

job_title: Social Policy & Social Welfare in the Canadian Context
application_procedure: Please apply by email, with a cover letter and curriculum vitae to angela.umbrello@utoronto.ca
duties: Teaching a course titled “Social Policy & Social Welfare in the Canadian Context ” based on the approved course outline.  The instructor must adhere to course policies on teaching and holding the course at the scheduled time.  The course content, notes, readings, and assignments are uniform across all sections.  The teaching team meets regularly to discuss teaching and learning activities and instructors are expected to attend these meetings.  For all assignments and final grades, instructors are expected to meet established deadlines. It is important to have consistency across all sections and the course coordinator is available to support new instructors.  Quercus management: including learning and utilizing Quercus; the marking and grading of student work and the submission of grades to the course coordinator by the required date; adhering to the policies on teaching and holding classes when scheduled; the maintenance of reasonable hours of student contact outside of scheduled contact hours.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: PhD completed or in progress in social work or related field.  Experience in teaching Social Policy in the Canadian Context.   Preference may be given to candidates who have the most experience teaching in this area at the graduate level.
qualifications_preferred: PhD completed or in progress in social work (preferred) or related field.  Experience in teaching Social Policy in the Canadian Context.   Preference may be given to candidates who have the most experience teaching in this area at the graduate level.

department: Undergraduate Medical Education (Temerty Faculty of Medicine)
application_procedure: Please send the following to md.foundations@utoronto.ca before closing date (July 10, 2026 at 11:59pm): Subject line: Assistant Invigilator Positions (CUPE 3902); Up-to-date resume (cover letter is optional); Student number; Confirmation of registration status on ACORN; Confirmation of availability for the period outlined above; Incomplete applications will not be considered.
duties: Responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following:  Supervising in-person assessments (i.e. Mastery Exercises and Progress Tests) across the MD Program; some remote shifts may be required; Through active invigilation, maintain the integrity of assessments by adhering to faculty and university policies and procedures. Maintaining confidentiality and security of student and assessment information; Monitoring test/exam time allotments; Handling student concerns/problems and referring to MD Program staff where appropriate; Reporting anomalous situations; Verifying student identification; Making assessment related announcements to students; In certain cases, invigilators may assist in transporting backup laptops between the Medical Sciences Building and the Exam Center at 255 McCaul Street or another exam site on the St. George campus; Invigilators are required to attend an in-person orientation session for a mock Mastery Exercise August 24, 2026.
qualifications: Applicants must be currently registered as students pursuing a graduate degree with the School of Graduate Studies at the University of Toronto; Applicants must be available for in-person invigilation shifts between August 24, 2026 and June 30, 2027; Applicants must be available for virtual orientation week of August 17, 2026; Experience invigilating tests/exams is an asset; Understanding and ability to apply relevant University and government policies, procedures and legislation.

application_procedure: Applicants must submit a cover letter, C.V. or resume, and a list of references by July 6, 2026. Applicant cover letters should indicate up to three PART modules they are most suited to lead. Applications should be sent via email to the Robert Gillespie Academic Skills Centre at academicskills.utm@utoronto.ca with the subject title: PART Unit 1 Job Posting.
duties:
As a Teaching Assistant for the Robert Gillespie Academic Skills Centre’s (RGASC) Program for Accessing Research Training (PART), the TA will design and lead a select number of modules related to introductory research skills. The TA will perform the following duties: develop and instruct assigned in-person and/or online asynchronous and/or synchronous modules, mark assignments, track module attendance, and answer module-specific student inquiries. The TA will work and meet regularly with the RGASC Director and RGASC staff. Modules to be facilitated by successful applicants include: 

-Conducting Interviews 

-Descriptive Statistics 

-Inferential Statistics 

-Interdisciplinary Special Topic 

-Lab Safety and Conduct in Labs 

-Leading a Focus Group 

-Special Topics in STEM 

-Special Topics in the Humanities 

-Special Topics in the Social Sciences 

-Survey Techniques 

-Transcribing and Qualitative Coding 

Duties of this position shall be performed at the campus on which the position is located or online using Zoom.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Applicants must be currently enrolled as a Master or PhD graduate student at the University of Toronto. Applicants should have at least two years of experience as a Teaching Assistant at the University of Toronto and demonstrate strong facilitation and presentation skills and/or experience leading/facilitating workshops (e.g., tutorials, practicals, courses, etc.). Applicants must have experience conducting qualitative and/or quantitative research at the graduate level. Applicants must have the ability to work effectively with and show tact when communicating with PART administrators, students, and other Teaching Assistants.
qualifications_preferred: Preference will be given to candidates with experience developing and leading PART modules and/or teaching experience in an undergraduate research methods course. Completion of a graduate-level research methods course is an asset. Experience using Quercus is also an asset.

job_title: An Introduction to Statistical Reasoning and Data Science
application_procedure:
Application information is available at https://www.statistics.utoronto.ca/employment-opportunities/cupe-positions-unit-1. 
The deadline to submit your application is September 26, 2022. For more information, you may contact: Angela Niazmand, Undergraduate and TA Administrator, Department of Statistical Sciences, University of Toronto
700 University Ave- Rm 9160- 6, Ontario Power Building, Toronto, ON M5G 1Z5, www.statistics.utoronto.ca, E-mail: jobapps.statistics@utoronto.ca

If during the application and/or selection process you require accommodation due to a disability, please contact
jobapps.statistics@utoronto.ca
This job is posted in accordance with the CUPE 3
duties:
Duties are in person unless otherwise indicated. Duties will include any or all of the following: attending lectures, running 
tutorial sessions, facilitating and evaluating student engagement on on-line discussion boards, participating in training 
sessions, meeting with the instructors, holding office hours, grading student homework including written reports, and 
assisting with grading of tests and exams.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than previous experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Senior undergraduate students (third and fourth year) with strong background in statistics and exemplary communication skills may also be considered.
qualifications_preferred: Graduate student with strong background in statistics, as well as excellent communication and writing skills, and the ability to interact well with undergraduate students.
